description:
You are assisting a data-science researcher who wants a lightning-fast way to bundle a set of CSV datasets for transfer without wasting CPU time on compression.

Current situation  
• Two CSV files already exist under /home/user/datasets/raw  
  – /home/user/datasets/raw/iris.csv  
  – /home/user/datasets/raw/mtcars.csv

Goal  
1. Create a single *uncompressed* tar archive that contains every “*.csv” file located anywhere inside /home/user/datasets/raw.  
   • Path of the archive: /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.tar  
   • Do NOT apply gzip, bzip2, xz, or any other compression option; the archive must be plain tar to save CPU cycles.  
   • The raw directory and its files must remain intact after the archive is made.  

2. Immediately compute the MD5 checksum of the new archive and store the result in a one-line text file.  
   • Path of the checksum file: /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.md5  
   • File format (exactly one line, two spaces between the hash and the filename, no trailing spaces or newlines after the line):  
     <32-character-hexadecimal-hash><two spaces>datasets_archive.tar  
   • Example of correct formatting (hash shown is only a placeholder):  
     d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e  datasets_archive.tar  

Performance note  
Creating an uncompressed tarball followed by a checksum requires only two quick disk-bound operations and avoids the CPU overhead of compression algorithms—this is the optimization the researcher wants.

Success criteria checked by the automated grader  
• /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.tar exists and is an uncompressed tar archive.  
• The tar archive contains exactly the files iris.csv and mtcars.csv at the path raw/ inside the archive.  
• /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.md5 exists and contains exactly one line in the format described above.  
• The MD5 checksum written in datasets_archive.md5 matches the archive’s actual MD5 checksum.  

No other files or directories should be modified or created.  
You can accomplish everything with just two or three standard Linux commands.
truth:
Initial files that already exist when the task starts  
• /home/user/datasets/raw/iris.csv  
  Contents (comma-separated, 5 sample lines):  
  sepal_length,sepal_width,petal_length,petal_width,species  
  5.1,3.5,1.4,0.2,setosa  
  4.9,3.0,1.4,0.2,setosa  
  4.7,3.2,1.3,0.2,setosa  
  4.6,3.1,1.5,0.2,setosa  

• /home/user/datasets/raw/mtcars.csv  
  Contents (comma-separated, 5 sample lines):  
  model,mpg,cyl,disp,hp,drat,wt,qsec,vs,am,gear,carb  
  Mazda RX4,21.0,6,160.0,110,3.90,2.620,16.46,0,1,4,4  
  Mazda RX4 Wag,21.0,6,160.0,110,3.90,2.875,17.02,0,1,4,4  
  Datsun 710,22.8,4,108.0,93,3.85,2.320,18.61,1,1,4,1  
  Hornet 4 Drive,21.4,6,258.0,110,3.08,3.215,19.44,1,0,3,1  

Files that must exist after successful execution  
1. /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.tar  
   • Plain (uncompressed) POSIX ustar archive whose root entries are:  
     raw/  
     raw/iris.csv  
     raw/mtcars.csv  

2. /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.md5  
   • Single line in the exact format “<md5><two spaces>datasets_archive.tar”  
   • The value <md5> must be the MD5 hash of /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.tar created in this run.  

Automated tests will:  
• Ensure datasets_archive.tar is an uncompressed tar archive by inspecting the magic bytes (0x75, 0x73, 0x74, 0x61, 0x72, “ustar”).  
• List the archive’s table of contents and verify only the two CSV files (prefixed by raw/) are present.  
• Compute md5sum /home/user/datasets/datasets_archive.tar and compare it with the value stored in datasets_archive.md5; they must be identical.  
• Fail the task if any compression (e.g., .gz) is detected, if additional files are in the tarball, or if formatting of the MD5 file deviates from specification.
